And I think Dan hit it on a couple quick points that we're not covering in this, but really it's lead flow, right? So it's the, it's the little- That's why this ties in perfectly with magnetic marketing and- And EIMC and- Ultimate lead generation machine, yeah. Because, you know, the lazy salesperson or the desperate salesperson thinks, just get me in front of somebody and I'm going to get them close. Well, and- And I have to. Right, right, yeah. And they're, and it's just not going to work. And it's going to be, it's going to be unfortunate for everyone to say the least. And it's going to be bumpy and ugly and it's just not, yeah. So you have to be in a position where you know, not hope, not by positive attitude, have faith in, you have to know there's another bus coming. Right? So for some reason, this bus and I are not, get away from the bus stop because there's another bus coming. And if you don't have yourself in that position as a salesperson, then you are always going to surrender effective selling in favor of desperation. Next objection is, and we've actually covered this one right here, this won't work for me, this won't work in my business. That's one that we hear all the time at GKIC, again, almost. Well, the consumer versions are, this won't work for me because we're Catholic, we're Mormon, we're fat, we're old, we're young. We live in the South, we live in the Midwest. Yeah, so again, this is something that in my mind should be handled way in advance. I mean, if you're getting this objection during a presentation, I mean, shouldn't you be handling this in advance? I think it's best to handle testimonials, right? So if you know who they are, you would say, these are doctors that use this if the person says it's not going to work for my medical practice. That's really where it's, proof and abundance with your testimonials is the best way to do that. Goes back to really taking the time and the thought to work your way through the script. Yeah, so there's a marketing before selling opportunity to match people with social proof, corporate proof, whatever, right? So in selling Oklahoma, one of the kinds of people who would not normally want to locate in Oklahoma. Excellent from the big city, like New York City. Yeah, or the tech guys. Yeah, tech guys. However, Google has a big plant there. Right, you know, so to them, you should be telling the Google story, right? To retailers, Macy's has a big distribution center there and Amazon has a big distribution center. So the sloppy thing is to show everybody, everything to everybody, right? Which is woefully ineffective, right? Cause I'm most influenced by somebody who looks like me, talks like me, walks like me, et cetera. So there's a way to start to deal with this long before it happens in selling. In the sales place, you better be ready. You better be able to produce that social proof as it is needed to match to that person. What about hidden objections? Let's talk a little bit about one of them, which is I believe these testimonials, okay? I believe that you're the real deal, but I don't believe in myself. I don't trust in myself. They're not gonna, I think very few people are gonna come out and say, hey, I don't think I can do it. How do we handle that as a hidden objection? You know, I think that's valid. I mean, a lot of times people will not trust themselves to implement. If it's a marketing campaign, they may not think that they could implement all 52 steps. If it's a diet, they think they won't stick to it. They are, you have to think about it. They are plagued with their history of positive and negative, but mostly they're thinking about their failures. Most likely not. Right, and it's the diets that didn't work. The ghosts of disappointments passed. The ads that didn't pull. Come running out of the closet. And I think that's valid. And I think the best way to do it is take them off the hook for that. And you can say, I realize in the past, maybe so-and-so didn't go the way we wanted to, but these are the reasons why we've created this foolproof mechanism. And I think you have to take them off the hook. You have to tell them it's not their fault about the past in a way that it's appropriate. And then you have to just move forward. But it is, I think of all the valid objections, it's probably one of the most valid. So I'm writing a copy right now for Roxanne Emmerich. Who you got? Not sure. Titanium member. Titanium member. Minnesota. And deals with bankers. Bank presidents, bank CEOs. With complete culture transformation, touching really every aspect of the bank.
